speaker: interviewer
text: We're continuing now with tape 3 of the interview of Eva Weiner. We're talking-- the war ended. Do you know about when that was, and how did they know the war had ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: How the war end?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when was that? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- 1944, 1944 I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who had come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-- they came to Rivne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Russians when you came into Rivne then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I came to Rivne from hiding, the Russian was there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to Rivne. So the first thing where I went is where my homes was. And they wasn't bombed. And they was occupied by Polish people and Russian people. And I came in. And of course, I was blown up like a balloon with the swollen legs, with a swollen face. They didn't know my sister, but I had the [INAUDIBLE] so everybody knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said the first thing in Polish. Are you still alive? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, God gives you some-- you get so smart. I said, I came to tell you to get out of here. It's my house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah? We're going to call, and we're going to tell them. I said, who are you going to call? Hitler? Hitler is dead. The Russian? You'll tell the Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go tell them so they'll give me-- they'll take you out this minute. It's my home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, they, they didn't argue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only took one, one, one house for myself and my daughter. But when I came, was already common. Do you know what means [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Jewish people where they was in the woods fighting with the German, putting bombs under the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stations, under the bricks. They was fighting, and they called them [NON-ENGLISH]. So they was [INAUDIBLE]. And they was looking who is [INAUDIBLE]. And when they heard, there came two women with the children. And they had their house, so I don't have to tell you. They all came to me. They all came running around. First day, two days, still they gave me their apartment-- took a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they was having occupied already a big house with a couple homes and everything. And we was there, and we had the children there. And of course we didn't have-- looking for food for this or that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'll never forget was then a bottle of gas. And my daughter was thinking it's water. She grabbed the bottle and drank the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't have to tell you how we was running to the hospital to pump out the gas of her. And that was a life. It was a life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody helped each other. It was no problem. For bread, I worked potatoes. This one worked a mill. This one got that. We was together. All of us was together. And then we was happy we survived. And then we was talking what we're going to do for Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did nothing. What can we do for Hitler? And then we finded out what they did to Warsaw ghetto and all those things. It was very, very exciting, very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because the Russian came in and there was [INAUDIBLE] some day, was nice. Some day wasn't so nice. Some day, look, when they got a young, nice girl, they raped her. It was, it was not pleasant life. But we managed to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any news of your husband at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my husband was with us around at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that your husband came back with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He ran away from the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did, when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came-- when we came to Rivne. And he was asking around. He was going around. He's gone around. He was-- but the Russian army, the, the Polish army-- the Russian took it over. And when they announced all the Polish citizens can go back to Poland. So he came that time. And he started to ask around, started to look. It took a nice couple months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was it that he got back with you? What season?

speaker: interviewee
text: What season-- was in the summertime. But I really can't recall what year. I really can't-- '40 or '41 or '39, something in between these three years. I can't remember, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All killed-- everybody was killed. But I know my brother David was taken to the Russian army and my other two brothers one-- so I started to write letters to the Russian committee to see where they are. And they started to look for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brothers find me before I find them. So one of the brothers came. David came. The others was killed. So out of the seven children, how many survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we're two-- me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: But David had survived the war as well? He lived through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the two sisters that were hiding. Then how long were you in Rivne for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Rivne until 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I go-- I went-- we went with the transport. They was taking-- the joint [INAUDIBLE] was taking us to-- back to Germany. Because they-- who wants to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who wants to go to America? Who has family? Who has-- so we went back to Germany, where that-- the war was finished. And where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] At first, we went-- we was living in Germany, in Pocking, Pocking. So we was living there I think. But-- no, we was living there. We first went to Passau. Then we went to [NON-ENGLISH] for a short time because I was very sick. I was so sick, I lost complete-- I was anemic, complete anemic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband was schlepping me from one hospital to another, from one to another. Finally, we settled down in Pocking. And we was in Pocking. There, we got the papers to go to America. How did you get the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what my husband did, he used to go almost every other day to Munchen. Not [INAUDIBLE], travel to Munchen. And he remembers his mother and his father used to talk about their brothers there in America. And their name is Weiner. So he was taking every Weiner in the telephone book and come home and write letters. He was writing Polish, Latin, ABC, and in Polish. And he sent to every Weiner in America. I think it was something what he picked it up. He was-- he would do to the last minute. But I think about 49 or 52 Weiners. And one of the Weiners sent us a letter. And that was his father's brother. And they brought us to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know which city they were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he would know, he wouldn't have-- he sent letters to Philadelphia, to New York, to Chicago, to Detroit-- all over where he saw a Weiner. And they was living in Philadelphia. And when my cousin was reading the letter to my uncle-- he was a rather old man-- he said, oh, no, and he got hysterical. That's my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's my brother's son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and they said to him, Pop, that's not so. Everybody writes now they cousins and they nephews and they brothers because they want to come to America. But that's not your nephew. It's not your brother's son and stop bothering. So they didn't-- he didn't let them go for a couple months. Finally, he said, if you're not going to write them a letter, I'm going to kill myself. That's my brother's son. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because he told them the names from his sisters, the names from his brothers, the names from my uncle, [INAUDIBLE] uncle's brothers in every detail. And he knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew it's his nephew. So that's the way they send us the letter. And we wrote back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start to write to them. And they sent us the papers. And we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was ready to go to Israel. We was packed to go to Israel. My husband was working underground. He always was very active. And he came to take us, me and my daughter. And she had the mumps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our clothes and everything was already gone. And he came in just-- we were sitting like now. I'm sitting and waiting. And she got the mumps-- the big, swollen mumps. And we couldn't go. That's the way we remained. And we wrote a letter again to them. And we told them we didn't go to Israel. We still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my uncle from Israel wanted to send a person to take my daughter to Israel. And then we got anywhere-- we'll be able to go to America, to Israel. But I wanted to go to, to Israel. So he wrote a letter. He's sending a person to take my daughter to Israel. So I tell him, where I go, she goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not going nowhere. And that's the way we came to America. We didn't go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here Columbus Day, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell anybody about your experiences in Europe when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't ever have that to tell the story of what I've been through. I tell them I'm a survivor, and I don't want to talk about it. But one [INAUDIBLE] the book.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very hard for me. It's a couple years already. I'm getting, I guess, older. I don't know, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard for me to tell the whole thing. I told, I told her, if you want slightly more or less, I can do it. But I can't get in deep to all the details. Because it's very hard for me. I'm very emotional. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this way, when I talk to my friends, to my card players or to anyone, oh, she's a survivor. She's great. She's getting a lot of money from Germany. We don't get-- we never get paid what we went through. We never get paid what we left-- never, never. I'll tell you, if I get my check, most of the time-- now my husband died [INAUDIBLE]. I'd give a lot of donations because I don't want Hitler's money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband was working, making a nice living, and I don't need it. Israel needs-- my daughter just donated Israel a lump of money. They're building a, a wing from the, a hospital for the children. So in Greensboro they-- so much and so much you have to give. And they have in their name Greensboro Wing hospital for the children. So I and my daughter were very charitable, what we can do for anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my oldest one is Donna. I call her [INAUDIBLE]. Debbie-- I call her [INAUDIBLE]. And Julie is [INAUDIBLE], very, very good children, very intelligent, thank God. I always used to say to my daughter, what I couldn't give you, please, let's give the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're all professional.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about any great-grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have [INAUDIBLE] another five of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one is Paula, Dara, and Adam. That's for my oldest granddaughter. Then from my middle one is Charlie and Michelle. And Michelle is named after my husband. His name was Meyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything about your experiences during the war that I have not asked you that you'd like to tell me about?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war? Why--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything that I haven't asked you about that you'd like to include in your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lot to include, but I prefer even not to talk about it because it get so emotional. I can, I can started to tell what we went through in the war. It's, it's-- you have to be living with the person and not to hurt them day by day to get a little bit of it. But I'm not ready to talk about it. I can't talk about it. And when we came after the war, I saw what I wanted. I said-- I talked to God. I believe in God. I said, God, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] give my daughter a home. [SOBBING] You can take me anytime you want to. And I guess God believes I mean with all my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave my daughter a wonderful, wonderful husband and very good three children. And I have a big pleasure of it. And now it's my whole life involved with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope you understand that I speak English like, like a newcomer. What can I tell you? If there was one thing you could say to people who had not experienced the Holocaust, what would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not experienced?

speaker: interviewer
text: People from future generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know the meaning of it. A lot of people don't believe it. My daughter goes to schools, to colleges, to talk about Holocaust. And where my daughter lives in Greensboro, North Carolina, is a Gentile state, city. They touch her after she has the speech, and they ask her if it's-- if she's a lie or if it's a racket in a, in a [INAUDIBLE] talks. They don't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't believe it. She's alive, and she talks about it. And matter of fact, they had from Anna-- what was her name? In Greensboro they-- her life story, Anna-- she wrote the book. Anna [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And my daughter arranged it. You know, Anna-- the girl, what she wrote the book. If I can-- you don't know? Franklin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Anna Franklin. Right. She had them use [INAUDIBLE] but they all came to Greensboro. It was for two weeks. I was talking there and she was-- she's arranging all those things. My daughter's a big, big worker for the Holocaust and for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished-- I'm finished. Thank you, I appreciate, and I'm sorry if I broke down. I can't help it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't help it. It's one of those things. You know what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [INAUDIBLE] life. It's [INAUDIBLE] and my eyes will close. You can never forget. And those dreams what you have, you wake up and you say, is it-- I'm not there. You're not running. You're not hiding. They don't run after you with the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's, it's-- you can't forget it. You can't. You try. You try your best. You never forget it. And I hope and pray to God. I'm in a golden place, my golden [INAUDIBLE] in America. And the American people appreciate the country where they live. Because nowhere on this earth is a country like America. [INAUDIBLE] she'll find these three pictures and share [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest sister, Rivka, and my youngest brother, David. Was taken about when?

speaker: interviewee
text: About '29 or '30, 1939 or--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivne. This is the picture from my oldest brother. His name is Arzek. And was taken in Rivne but before the war, in 1930 or '31. And he was in the-- when he was taken before the war to the Russian army when they came in when Hitler-- before Hitler came in. So that's when was taken the picture. This is-- the picture is-- I am, I am to the left. My name is Chawa. And this is my cousin. Her name is Ruchel. And it's taken in 19-- this I remember-- in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivne. You going to-- to the right is my sister. She's a survivor. And in the middle is my brother, what is a survivor. And next to him is my niece, my sister's daughter, and she's a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me their names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Sonja, and my brother's name is Shlomo, and my niece's name is Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1980s-- '96-- '69. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] To the right is my sister Sonja. And the picture is taken in Leningrad in 1969. And in the middle is my brother, a survivor, Shlomo, and taken the picture in Leningrad at the same time. And next to my brother is my niece, my sister's daughter. And her name is Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- OK, we agreed. And wait-- let's-- I was going to say we'll agree when the picture was taken before we start so it's easy. OK. OK, so tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is is my husband, Meyer. Next to him is my son-in-law, [INAUDIBLE], Frank. Next to Frank is Joe, my granddaughter's husband. And next to Joe is Jeff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their last names also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeff Cohen and Joe Friedman and my son-in-law is Weiner, and my husband is Weiner. She met another Weiner. Next to my son-in-law, Jeff Cohen is my daughter Rochelle Weiner and her oldest daughter, Donna Friedman, and Julie Weiner. She's single. She's not married. And next to Julie is-- I am. My name is Eva Weiner. And now this is my middle great-granddaughter Dara Friedman. And then is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Paula Friedman. I'm sorry. And then her mother holding her is Dara Friedman. And the little one is Charlie Cohen. And this is my middle granddaughter, Debbie Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's five years old. So it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: '91. That's-- 1991 in North Carolina, Greensboro.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gloria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great-granddaughter, Michelle. She's named after my husband. My husband's going to be two years dead. And in Hebrew name is her name, Myra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her last name is Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken about five, six weeks ago this year. To the left is my first-born boy in our family. His name is Adam. And he's about two and a half years old-- Adam Friedman. Next is my oldest great-granddaughter, Paula Friedman. And it was taken in North Carolina, Greensboro. And the third one is Dara Friedman. And she's about four and a half years old. And taken in North Carolina, Greensboro.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1995.
